title: Law Enforcement and Autism Interaction Dataset
description: Comprehensive compilation of statistics, cases, trends, and outcomes regarding interactions between law enforcement and autistic individuals
data_sources: Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders (2016)

fatal_encounters.name: Ryan Gainer
fatal_encounters.details: Shot and killed by sheriff's deputy outside his home after family called 911 for help during behavioral crisis. Bodycam footage showed teen had chased an officer with a gardening tool, but family had informed deputies of his autism and he had already calmed down before police arrived.

fatal_encounters.name: Osaze Osagie
fatal_encounters.details: Fatally shot by police during mental health check. His parents had previously alerted authorities about his condition. When Osagie emerged holding a knife, officers attempted less-lethal subduing and then opened fire. No charges filed against officers, family later sued.
fatal_encounters.quote: Men with guns showed up and gave him a million instructions…it shouldn't be this way

fatal_encounters.name: Ethan Saylor
fatal_encounters.details: Young man with Down syndrome who died in police custody. Media initially blamed victim's disability rather than scrutinizing officers' actions.

use_of_force_incidents.name: Linden Cameron
use_of_force_incidents.details: Shot multiple times by Salt Lake City police after mother called 911 seeking help during mental health meltdown. Officers were told he was unarmed and having a crisis, yet shortly after arriving they shouted commands and fired multiple shots as the panicked child fled.

use_of_force_incidents.name: Arnaldo Rios Soto
use_of_force_incidents.details: Case where police shot the unarmed caregiver of an autistic man, despite officers having undergone disability training.

wrongful_detainments.name: Reginald "Neli" Latson
wrongful_detainments.details: Waiting outside public library when someone called police about a 'suspicious' person. Officer's attempt to detain Neli led to physical altercation - likely a fight-or-flight reaction related to his autism. Arrested and convicted of assaulting an officer. Served time in prison with long stretches in solitary confinement.
wrongful_detainments.outcome: Received conditional pardon in 2015 (to transfer to treatment setting) and full pardon in 2021
wrongful_detainments.advocacy: ACLU noted case exemplified systemic failures: lack of police training and overuse of jails instead of community services

wrongful_detainments.name: Matthew Rushin
wrongful_detainments.details: Involved in serious traffic accident. Police misunderstood his echolalia (repeating words, a coping mechanism) as evidence of criminal intent. Charged with attempted murder and other felonies despite questions about his intent and possible medical episode causing the crash.
wrongful_detainments.outcome: Pled guilty under pressure and sentenced to 10 years in prison. Received conditional pardon in 2021 after over 2 years behind bars. Conviction still stands, family seeks full exoneration.

by_age_21.interpretation: 1 in 5 autistic young adults have had police contact by their early twenties
by_age_21.source: Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders study (2016)

comparison_to_general_population.source: U.S. DOJ data (cited by Marshall Project)

international.uk_data: 18% of autistic adults had been stopped or arrested by police in their lifetime

incarceration.source: Prison Policy Initiative; The Arc estimate

gender_disparity.female_victimization: Higher odds of being crime victims

risk_factors.co_occurring_conditions: Having a co-occurring psychiatric condition increased odds of justice system interaction by nearly 2.7 times

regional_data.san_diego_county: 81% of those shot in officer-involved shootings had either a mental health condition or substance use disorder

racial_disparities.black_americans: 2-3 times more likely to be killed by police than white Americans

traffic_stop_initiatives.name: Blue Envelope Program
traffic_stop_initiatives.description: Drivers keep license, registration, and autism information card in distinct blue envelope. Instructs both driver and officer on navigating the stop - alerting officer that individual has autism and may not respond typically.
traffic_stop_initiatives.purpose: Prevent escalation by immediately signaling to officer that driver may have communication challenges and sensory sensitivities

traffic_stop_initiatives.name: Autism License Designations
traffic_stop_initiatives.description: Voluntary autism notations on driver's licenses or state IDs
traffic_stop_initiatives.purpose: Empower autistic drivers to disclose their condition, prompting officers to adjust tactics (giving extra time to respond, avoiding bright lights or loud commands)

traffic_stop_initiatives.name: Vehicle Placards/Decals
traffic_stop_initiatives.description: Visual indicators for vehicles indicating an occupant's autism diagnosis
traffic_stop_initiatives.purpose: Quick visual identification for officers during traffic stops

state_mandates.interpretation: Well over half of U.S. states do not require police to be trained in how to recognize or interact with autistic or developmentally disabled individuals
state_mandates.source: ABC News / NCSL; Marshall Project; PBS NewsHour

states_with_recent_legislation.description: Enacted autism training requirement for officers

states_with_recent_legislation.status: Bill to expand autism training died in committee in 2023
Considered legislation, including special designation on driver's licenses

states_with_recent_legislation.description: Moved to make blue envelopes available statewide through legislation
Program allowing families to voluntarily provide information about autistic household member to local police

states_with_recent_legislation.status: Now mandates training on ASD for officers (as of 2024)

community_initiatives.description: Caregivers register person with autism and provide information like triggers, de-escalation strategies, or contact info. If call comes in involving that individual or address, dispatchers can alert responding officers.
community_initiatives.legislation: Illinois 2016 law encouraged creation of such databases

community_initiatives.description: Programs pairing mental health professionals with police on certain calls
Unarmed mobile crisis units to handle nonviolent mental health emergencies instead of police
community_initiatives.endorsement: U.S. Department of Justice recently endorsed efforts to reduce police involvement in mental health calls, calling traditional police responses 'less effective, potentially harmful'

community_initiatives.description: Mental illness-focused training that often includes segments on autism and developmental disabilities
community_initiatives.effectiveness_note: Data on effectiveness is limited; many use-of-force databases do not capture whether subject had autism or disability

community_initiatives.description: Some agencies instruct officers to reduce sensory stimuli when possible - turning off sirens or flashing lights if safe to do so - during autism-related calls

demographic: Male gender (2× the risk of police interaction compared to females)
Being Black or from other marginalized communities (double jeopardy of bias)
Co-occurring psychiatric conditions (increased odds by 2.7 times)
Young autistic men, especially those with additional mental health needs, at highest risk of police encounters
behavioral: Avoiding eye contact (police trained to notice as 'suspicious')
Delayed responses to questions or commands
Echolalia (repeating words) misinterpreted as admission of guilt or intent
misinterpretation_patterns: Non-compliance often actually inability to understand or swiftly follow orders
What looks like resisting arrest may be sensory overload or communication gap
Fight-or-flight reactions related to autism misread as aggression

key_positions: Emphasize autistic people themselves should have say in reforms
Highlight pattern of excessive force against people with disabilities, especially people of color with disabilities
Critique mere 'disability training' as sole solution - training alone hasn't prevented tragedies
Call for greater police accountability, data collection on use of force incidents involving disabilities
proposed_solutions: Deeper changes in police culture and protocols beyond one-time training
ASAN.source: Autistic Self Advocacy Network (ASAN) - Autism & Safety Toolkit: Research Overview (2017)

key_positions: Jails and prisons should not be default management for autism or mental health
States must provide suitable community services rather than using criminal justice system to 'warehouse' people with disabilities
actions: Involved in cases like Neli Latson's
2022 lawsuit in Washington, D.C. arguing dispatching armed police to mental health crises violates Americans with Disabilities Act
Using disability rights law to demand better training and alternative response options
ACLU.source: ACLU of Virginia - Press Release on Neli Latson Pardon (Jan. 20, 2015)

key_positions: Promote identification tools (autism ID cards, vehicle decals, license notations)
Respect that disclosure should be voluntary - some fear stigma or targeting
initiatives: Collaboration with law enforcement on training curricula
Programs like Project Lifesaver (for wandering prevention)
Autism_Speaks.source: Autism Speaks - 'Autism and driving: Ways to identify as autistic when driving' (2022)

key_positions: Seth Stoughton (law professor/former officer): Police training needs to reframe 'compliance' when dealing with disabled civilians
Lack of eye contact or failure to obey often not willful resistance but a cue to de-escalate
research_focus: Identifying risk factors to target preventive support
recommendations: Implementing standardized protocols (checking for medical ID jewelry or wallet cards before assuming noncompliance)
Including autistic people in training of police through direct storytelling or videos
Building empathy by having officers understand how sirens or shouted orders feel to person with autism

training_gaps.national_standards: No consistent national standards or funding for autism training
training_gaps.effectiveness_measurement: Very difficult to collect data on how many encounters officers have with autistic people and whether training improves outcomes
training_gaps.implementation: Training needs to be more widespread and scenario-based, coupled with departmental policies emphasizing de-escalation

media_coverage.bias: Initial media narratives often blame individual's disability for outcome rather than scrutinizing officers' actions
media_coverage.framing: Portray incidents as 'tragic accidents' inherent to autism rather than examining police protocols

justice_system.false_confessions: Autistic individuals have falsely confessed or gotten entangled in justice system because they did not understand rights or implications of police questioning
justice_system.court_disadvantages: U.K. study noted autistic adults sometimes disadvantaged during police interrogations and court due to communication differences and juries misreading their affect
justice_system.overrepresentation: People with cognitive and developmental disabilities significantly overrepresented in prisons

response_models.traditional_approach: Typical police tools (loud commands, bright lights, physical restraints) can exacerbate autistic person's distress
response_models.alternative_paradigm: Growing support for alternative crisis response teams to handle nonviolent mental health emergencies instead of police
